text: This is Dr. Craig Rogers, Director of Renal Surgery at the Vatakuta Urology Institute. In this video, we demonstrate techniques of renal hilar clamping.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to place the renal hilar vessels on stretch, facilitating dissection and clamping. The ProGrasp instrument in the fourth arm is freed to apply robotic bulldog clamps to the vessels.

The assistant introduces the robotic bulldog clamp into the abdomen, which the surgeon then grasps using the robotic ProGrasp instrument. The surgeon positions the clamp on the renal artery, demonstrating control over its placement.
The surgeon clamps the renal vein to enhance visualization during the procedure, particularly for more complex tumors.

The surgeon uses monopolar curved scissors to begin excising the tumor along the previously scored mark on the renal capsule. The incision is made circumferentially around the tumor, including a margin of normal renal parenchyma. The robotic grasping instrument is spread along the line of resection to provide retraction and enhance visualization for further resection.

The assistant uses a suction tip to clear blood from the surgical field and applies counter traction on the renal parenchyma to delineate the dissection plane. The robotic grasper provides counter traction on the tumor during resection, ensuring careful manipulation to avoid trauma.
The excised tumor is visibly set aside in the surgical field, awaiting retrieval at a later stage in the procedure.

The surgeon excises the upper pole tumor using monopolar curved scissors, following the previously scored mark on the renal capsule. The resection is performed circumferentially through the renal cortex, with upward traction on the tumor and spreading in the resection plane to aid in developing the proper resection plane.
The surgeon completes the excision of the tumor, placing it aside for later retrieval, with visible separation from the renal parenchyma.
In the next video we demonstrate the techniques of reconstructing the inner layer of the resection bed,
